title: Igor Jankov -Director's Interview - Megan | Managing Editor
meeting_title: Igor Jankov -Director's Interview - Megan | Managing Editor

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
All right.
So I'm pretty good.
Yeah, pretty good.
I just moved into a new house two days ago, so I'm still a little bit all over the place.
I'm getting settled in.
Nice.
Where do you live?
So I was around the Philadelphia side of Pennsylvania.
Now I moved to the other side, around Pittsburgh.
Okay.
Nice.
Cool.
Yeah.
Awesome.
It's my first home.
There's a lot to do.
You know, before this, I was in an apartment.
So it's lots of work.
Yeah.
Yeah, for sure.
Awesome.
Well, mean, that's super exciting.
So yeah, congrats.
How are you?
Where are you based from?
Yeah, I'm good.
I'm based in San Francisco.
Yeah, I like actually just got, I was traveling for Thanksgiving, so I like just got back into town yesterday.
But yeah, happy to be back home where there's like no snowstorm.
Right.
We just got, we just got one last night.
So.
Okay.
Yeah, because I was in, I was in.
And like, we just got like totally hit.
I'm like, yeah, but yeah, glad to have made it back.
But, but yeah, so a little bit about myself.
So I'm, I'm a director here at GrowthX.
I've been here at the company since about, I think May.

And yeah, we're kind of, yeah, GrowthX is kind of in, we're in a bit of like a transitional phase right now where we're kind of, kind of changing, kind of like changing like roles and responsibilities.
So whereas directors used to be more involved with like client facing work, now that's sort of changing.
And now like managing editors, they're really supposed to be involved doing like end-to-end like content production.
So whereas before we were, we had like content managers.
So yeah, it's definitely an exciting time, but yeah, lots of, lots of change.
So yeah, yeah, sounds exciting.
Yeah.
Yeah.
For sure.
But yeah, I guess just to jump in.
Yeah, like I'm hoping to hear from you like a time when maybe you were like managing multiple projects or accounts with completing deadlines and how you handled that and organized your work.
So yeah, sure.
Approved.
I think we kind of take on a similar workflow as in GrowthX, where one year title is service delivery, right?
But it's in essence a managing editor where we take on multiple clients, you know, anywhere from like six to 10 at a time, depending on what time of year it is, you know, how busy we get.

But yeah, each of these clients, my role would have been to develop the style guide, vet onboard editors, ensure quality assurance, review all of their work, you know, also communicate with the client about any feedback and then translate.
Like that, but also into the style guides.
And yeah, with multiple clients, I've always found it easy to like group similar clients.

Like if it's SEO automotive blogs, you know, I would group those similar clients and try to like, just for efficiency sake, I would like make templates, you know, and just like adjust when we get started with pilot projects, things like that.
Nice.
Yeah.
And, and could you tell me a little bit more about like, just like what the actual tools that you use and you're like in your like everyday processes?
So, yeah, we, I would say we use Google a lot, like the Google Sheets, Google dashboards, for tracking editor availability.
Um, we use, um, we have an internal, uh, knowledge base.
I think, I think for a growthx, that would be linear or notion.
One of those two that.
Um, it'd be both.
Okay.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's usually how it goes.
It's usually a combination.
We use Linear, Notion, and Airtable, and sometimes Google Sheets just for fun.
So yeah, which we're kind of trying to streamline, but yeah, we use a lot of different...
Yeah, lots of Google Sheets.
Google Sheets are proofed, too, especially for style guides.
But yeah, those are the basic tools that we use.
Also Slack for all of our communication.
Slack is vital.
Yeah, for sure.
Nice.
And so are you, it sounds like at your current role, are you, you're responsible for editing all of the content that's coming through for your batch of roughly a dozen clients or so?
Yeah, so it involves editing.
Usually when I edit, the most is when the pilot project is getting started.
So I edit to more so define all the style guide rules and how exactly the client wants content to look.
But then after that, I move more...

Or into a managing role where I, after we built a team of editors, I just ensure that all the content looks how it should, they're editing as they should, that the style guide is being enforced, deadlines, timelines, availabilities, I track that type of stuff, and also performance issues.
Okay, cool.
Awesome.
Nice.
Yeah.
And so would you be able to tell me about a time where like you caught a major issue with content before it shipped to a customer and what you caught and how you caught it?

Yeah, so, so I think one of the biggest ones where I caught this, where there was a big client involved where we getting a big workload, was a company that was translating the Chinese web novels to English.
was all machine translated too.
Before that, this client, I've never had experience with web novels.
I kind of thought it's just kind of like, you know, books, but online.
But no, it goes deeper than that.
These are books that are online that never end.
So it goes for like 700, 800, 900 chapters.
As long as it's popular, they keep writing, right?
Jesus, okay.
So by the time you get to like chapter 900 or something, it's really hard to keep track which character has what kind of history, what other characters, who said what to who.
Especially when you throw in machine translation into the mix.
You know, there's mistakes that slip through.
So when I was brought on board, about 50% of our chapters that we would send back would get some kind of feedback like, hey, this needed to be corrected, you know.
I started putting together for each one of these specific books, just large sheets going through the chapters about each connection between each character, each plot point.
So I would have editors reference these sheets while they're working and they drastically reduced how much we're getting inconsistency issues, which was the biggest part of me.
Nice.
Cool.
Yeah, that's, yeah.
Through, uh...
Yeah, meticulous documentation I found is a very good way to eliminate these kinds of issues.
Yeah, for sure.
Absolutely.
Yeah, well, I'm definitely going to go down a web novel rabbit hole after this.
Yeah, yeah, no, it just goes on.
You said some of these most popular ones, they don't end them, right?
So the writers just kind of think as they go.
Yeah, for sure.
Yeah.
And then if you, let's see.
So would you be able to describe a time when like a boss or even a client gave you some critical feedback and what that feedback was and then how you responded?
Well, I would say the same client, right?
So the critical feedback was the 50% feedback rate.
I would say negative feedback rate from the client at the point when he came in.
So I think that's a good example of that as well.
Yeah, for sure.
For sure.
What about from, like, a boss?
Nothing too major.
I do my time tracking when I first started Proof because it was my first job where it involved freelancers from around the world, right?
So my tracking at first was kind of all over the place, you know, just I was, I found myself trying to adjust my time when I work to the editors that are important to me.

So I would be online, like, 9 p.m., 10 p.m., you know, then tomorrow I be online, like, 8 a.m., you know, just, like, get in touch with these critical editors until I started actually having a more consistent, you know, I took that feedback on board and I decided to have a more consistent, rigid schedule and had more, like, editors conform to me rather than me conform to editors.
Yeah, for sure.
Awesome.
Cool.
Well, yeah, I mean, those were really the main questions that I had for you.
But do you, because I also know you've talked to a bunch of other folks on the growthx team, but do you have any questions for me?
Yeah, just like general questions about the role.
I would say like, how many accounts would a managing editor handle at once?
So, yeah, yeah.
I think the number that we've landed on is three.
So, yeah, so you'd be responsible end-to-end for three client accounts.
And then each account, it kind of depends, but like it could range from five posts a week up to like 26.
But even like that 26 per week will likely, those are more like programmatic SEO blog posts versus like more editorial.
So there's like, we just like have templates that.

We use for those, but even with that, like, I think we're in, when we renew contracts, we're going to sort of try and, like, reduce that, reduce some of those, like, higher numbers, just to make it all more, more manageable for folks.
Okay, that sounds good.
I think my experience of proof translates well to, to growthx, seeing as how it proved also really, you know, juggling multiple clients.
Yeah.
With their own, like, specific, you know, wants and needs and style guide preferences.
Yeah, for sure.
Nice.
I guess another, another question, how do teams collaborate at Growthx?
Like, as a managing editor, who would I work with most closely?
Yeah, so you would, you'd work with what we're calling, sorry, we, like, literally just implemented these changes yesterday.
Yeah, I've heard, yeah, yes.
No, take your time.
We're like, what are we calling people?
An engagement manager?
Yeah.
All Yeah, Yeah…
And so those are, so basically, yeah, the engagement manager would be, is ultimately responsible for, for like the client.
So it's like, you'd be working on like three, three client accounts, but then your engagement manager probably has like eight that they're generally overseeing.
And then like, so that means there's like a couple of other managing editors in that group working with that same engagement manager.
So, but you'd mostly be collaborating with that engagement manager.
Okay.
Okay.
Yeah.
That makes sense.
All right.
And just a general question, like, where do you see growthx a few years from now?
Yeah.
I think like where we're at right now, it's like, we're still like, like we're an AI workflows company, but we, I feel like right now are still pretty services heavy.
And, So, Yung.

I think we're trying to move more into a world where like instead of being like 95% like hands-on services for folks, we maybe want to move closer to like 70% hands-on and like the rest be like this like essentially offering like a self-serve platform for clients that like maybe don't need all that hands-on work.

So it's like right now our engineering team is like really just trying to like beef up our workflow production tool, our analytics platform, AI visibility so that we can also just sort of sell that product as like option as one option.
But then the other option is like selling us as like that full package.
So like being able to also like target folks that maybe want don't don't need as much of like a hands-on experience.
Okay, so they diversify a little bit.
Yeah, exactly.
All right.
Well, yeah, that's it.
That's it for my questions as well.
Okay, cool.
Great.
Well, it was really nice chatting with you, Igor.
It was good chatting with you as well.
Yeah, and I hope you have a good rest of your day.
All right.
Enjoy your day.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interviewing Igor Jankov for the Managing Editor role.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=67.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Relevant Experience:** As a "Service Delivery" lead at Proof, Igor managed 6–10 clients, handling end-to-end content production, editor management, and QA.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=123.0)
  - [**Problem-Solving:** Solved a 50% client feedback rate on a complex web novel project by creating detailed character/plot guides, proving his ability to fix systemic quality issues.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=321.0)
  - [**Role Alignment:** The GrowthX role (3 clients, 5–26 posts/wk) is a direct fit for Igor's experience, which includes managing high-volume programmatic content.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=550.0)
  - [**GrowthX Strategy:** Shifting from 95% services to a 70/30 services/self-serve model, leveraging a new production tool to diversify revenue and client options.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=697.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX's Strategic Shift

  - [GrowthX is transitioning from a services-heavy model to a hybrid one.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=697.0)
  - [**Goal:** Move from 95% services to a 70% services / 30% self-serve platform mix.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=697.0)
  - [**Rationale:** Diversify revenue and serve clients who don't need full-service support.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=697.0)
  - [**Focus:** Engineering is enhancing the production tool, analytics, and AI visibility to enable this shift.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=697.0)

### Managing Editor Role & Team Structure

  - [The Managing Editor (ME) role is now end-to-end content production.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=79.0)
  - [**Workload:** 3 client accounts, with weekly volume ranging from 5 editorial posts to 26 programmatic SEO posts.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=550.0)
  - [**Team:** MEs report to an Engagement Manager, who oversees \~8 total client accounts.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=634.0)
  - [**Tools:** GrowthX uses Linear, Notion, Airtable, Google Sheets, and Slack.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=203.0)

### Igor's Experience & Problem-Solving

  - [**Current Role (Proof):** "Service Delivery" lead, managing 6–10 clients.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=123.0)
  - [**Responsibilities:** Style guide creation, editor vetting, QA, and client feedback integration.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=141.0)
  - [**Workflow:** Groups similar clients (e.g., SEO automotive) to create efficiency templates.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=182.0)
  - [**Key Project (Web Novels):**](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=321.0)
      - [**Problem:** A 50% client feedback rate on machine-translated Chinese web novels.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=321.0)
      - [**Cause:** Inconsistent character/plot details across 900+ chapters.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=369.0)
      - [**Solution:** Created detailed character/plot guides for editors to reference.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=395.0)
      - [**Outcome:** Drastically reduced feedback and inconsistency issues.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=395.0)
  - [**Feedback Response (Time Tracking):**](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=482.0)
      - [**Feedback:** Inconsistent time tracking due to adjusting to global freelancer schedules.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=482.0)
      - [**Action:** Implemented a rigid schedule and required editors to conform, improving efficiency.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=482.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Megan:** Review interview notes and discuss with the hiring team.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=782.0)
  - [**GrowthX:** Inform Igor of the next steps in the hiring process.](https://fathom.video/share/qzpiBYxjyeeytV-uJxBTBXmX3DEMzbqp?tab=summary&timestamp=782.0)
